era_context: The mid-12th century CE was a period of Crusader retrenchment in the Levant, feudal consolidation in Western Europe, and the height of the Jin Dynasty's power in northern China. In Japan, the Heian period was in its final decades, with the rise of the samurai class and the Taira clan's dominance. The Islamic world experienced significant cultural production, particularly in Al-Andalus and the Ayyubid territories. This was also a period of technological advancement in Europe, including improvements in agricultural techniques and the expansion of trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (fragmentary sources)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Taira clan defeats Minamoto clan at the Battle of Heiji
events.description: The Taira clan, led by Taira no Kiyomori, defeated the Minamoto clan in the Heiji Rebellion, a brief but significant conflict in Japan. This victory consolidated Taira dominance over the imperial court and marked a major step toward the establishment of the Taira shogunate. The Heiji Rebellion arose from factional disputes within the imperial court and the growing power of military clans. The Taira's victory led to the execution or exile of key Minamoto leaders, though some, including the young Minamoto no Yoritomo, escaped and would later lead the Minamoto's resurgence.
key_figures: Minamoto no Yoshitomo (Minamoto clan leader, killed in the rebellion)
Minamoto no Yoritomo (young survivor, later founder of Kamakura shogunate)

sources.name: Genpei Jōsuiki (Chronicle of the Genpei War)

events.certainty_note: The Heiji Rebellion is well-attested in Japanese historical records and literary sources. Dating to 1160 is secure in the Japanese calendar system (Heiji 2).
cross_references: 1185-001 (Minamoto victory at Dan-no-ura, reversing Taira dominance)

events.title: Nur ad-Din Zengi consolidates Ayyubid power in Syria
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi (Nur ad-Din Mahmud), the Zengid ruler of Syria and northern Mesopotamia, continued to consolidate power and expand Ayyubid influence in the region. Nur ad-Din was a key figure in the early Crusades period, known for his military campaigns against the Crusader States and his patronage of Islamic institutions. His reign (1146–1174) saw the strengthening of Islamic military and administrative structures in the Levant, laying groundwork for Saladin's later campaigns. Nur ad-Din's court was a center of Islamic learning and culture.
key_figures: Nur ad-Din Zengi (Zengid ruler of Syria)
Saladin (rising military commander under Nur ad-Din's patronage)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's consolidation of power is well-documented, but no single dramatic event is reliably dated to 1160 specifically. The year falls within a period of gradual political consolidation.

events.title: Henry II of England establishes legal reforms
events.description: Henry II of England continued his program of legal and administrative reforms during the 1160s, establishing the foundations of English common law. These reforms included the expansion of royal courts, the development of standardized legal procedures, and the assertion of royal authority over feudal lords and the Church. The Assize of Clarendon (1166) was a key document in this process, establishing procedures for criminal justice. These reforms had lasting impact on the development of English law and governance.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, Duke of Normandy and Anjou)
Thomas Becket (Archbishop of Canterbury, in conflict with Henry II)
events.certainty_note: Henry II's legal reforms are well-documented in contemporary chronicles and legal documents. The Assize of Clarendon is dated to 1166, but the broader reform program spans the 1160s.

disconfirming_evidence: The Heiji Rebellion is sometimes dated to 1159 rather than 1160, depending on the calendar system used (Japanese lunar calendar vs. proleptic Gregorian). Modern scholarship generally accepts 1160 as the correct date in the Gregorian system. No major disconfirming evidence was identified for other events listed.
historiographic_note: The year 1160 CE is moderately well-documented in European and East Asian sources, but documentation is sparse for most other regions. European sources are dominated by monastic chronicles and royal records, creating a Western and ecclesiastical bias. Japanese sources are rich but often focus on court politics and military narratives. Islamic sources are abundant for the Levant and Egypt but thinner for other regions. The historical record for 1160 is reasonably complete for Europe, the Islamic world, and East Asia, but nearly absent for sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia.

graph_edges.note: Taira victory at Heiji led to Minamoto exile and later resurgence
Nur ad-Din's consolidation was part of a longer trajectory of Ayyubid expansion
Henry II's legal reforms and assertion of royal authority contributed to conflict with Thomas Becket
